title: Paediatric DKA
oneLiner: Acute metabolic emergency in children with type 1 diabetes (T1DM) – hyperglycaemia, ketonaemia, acidosis – risk of cerebral oedema within first hours.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Cerebral oedema** – the top killer in paediatric DKA. Single finding: *headache + GCS drop* (even 1–2 points) or *bradycardia + hypertension* (Cushing’s reflex). Must be anticipated, not responded to after the fact.
- **Hypokalaemia** – fatal arrhythmia if insulin is given without adequate potassium replacement. Single finding: *U waves on cardiac monitor* or *low K+ on blood gas* (<3.0 mmol/L before starting insulin). Rule out by checking baseline K+ before any insulin.
- **Hypoglycaemia** – rapid drop from aggressive insulin without dextrose backup. Single finding: *glucose <4 mmol/L on hourly BM* while insulin is still running. Prevent by switching to 10% dextrose when glucose reaches 14 mmol/L.
sections.Presentation:
- Polydipsia, polyuria, nocturia (or enuresis in toilet-trained children) – often weeks of symptoms
- Weight loss (can be dramatic)
- Vomiting, abdominal pain (may mimic surgical abdomen)
- Dehydration signs: dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, sunken eyes, prolonged capillary refill
- Kussmaul breathing – deep, sighing respirations (compensatory respiratory alkalosis)
- Fruity-smelling breath (acetone)
- Altered consciousness (ranging from drowsy to coma – indicate cerebral oedema or severe acidosis)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state (HHS)** – rare in children; differentiate: pH >7.3, HCO3 >15, low or absent ketones, very high glucose (>30–40 mmol/L) and osmolality
- **Sepsis (e.g., pneumonia, gastroenteritis)** – can cause acidosis and vomiting; differentiate: no marked polyuria/weight loss, ketones may be present but blood glucose normal
- **Surgical abdomen (appendicitis, intussusception)** – abdominal pain with vomiting; differentiate: check glucose and ketones – if elevated, DKA is likely the cause
- **Salicylate poisoning** – can cause metabolic acidosis with respiratory alkalosis and vomiting; differentiate: no hyperglycaemia, no ketones, history of ingestion
- **New-onset T1DM without DKA** – mild polyuria/polydipsia, normal pH/HCO3; just need insulin start as outpatient

**Splitter:** *Blood pH <7.3 + serum ketones >3 mmol/L = DKA, not HHS or sepsis.*
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour):**
    - Capillary blood glucose (BG) – always from fingerstick (if inaccurate, use venous)
    - Urine/serum ketones (bedside meter – β-hydroxybutyrate preferred; >1.5 mmol/L confirms ketosis)
    - ECG (look for U waves, peaked T waves – hyperkalaemia; flat/broad T waves – hypokalaemia)
    - Hourly neurological observations (GCS, pupil size/reaction, vital signs) – start immediately
- **Bloods:**
    - Venous blood gas (VBG) for pH, HCO3, base deficit – arteriorial not needed
    - Serum electrolytes: Na, K, Cl, Ca, Mg (corrected Na: Na + 2×(glucose−5.6)/5.6)
    - Urea, creatinine, osmolality (calculated or measured)
    - Full blood count (WBC may be raised – stress/demargination, not infection)
    - Blood cultures if febrile or shock
    - HbA1c (confirms T1DM diagnosis, but not needed acutely)
- **Imaging:** Not routine. Only if suspected cerebral oedema: CT head (after clinical stabilisation) to rule out other causes of neuro deterioration – but treatment for cerebral oedema should not wait for CT.
- **Diagnostic criteria (ISPAD/BSPED):**
    - Hyperglycaemia: plasma glucose >11 mmol/L
    - Acidosis: venous pH <7.3 OR HCO3 <15 mmol/L
    - Ketonaemia: serum β-hydroxybutyrate ≥3 mmol/L or urine ketones ≥3+
- **Never-miss test:** *Hourly GCS + pupillary exam* – the earliest sign of cerebral oedema. Also: *baseline potassium before starting insulin* – fatal hypokalaemia if ignored.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - **ABC:** Secure airway if GCS <8 or progressive deterioration. Give 100% oxygen if shocked/seizure.
    - **IV access:** Two wide-bore cannulae. Take blood before any fluid.
    - **Fluid resuscitation:**
        - If shocked (cap refill >2 s, weak pulses, tachycardia, hypotension): give 10 mL/kg 0.9% NaCl over 15–30 min. Reassess. Maximum 20 mL/kg unless severe shock.
        - Dehydration calculation: use clinical estimate (mild 5%, moderate 7.5%, severe 10%).
        - Deficit replaced over 48 hours – do NOT aim for rapid correction (risk of cerebral oedema). Use 0.9% NaCl ± 5% dextrose (once glucose <14 mmol/L).
        - Add potassium chloride [check local policy – typically 40 mmol/L unless K+ <2.5, then higher]
    - **Insulin:** Do NOT give IV bolus. Start IV infusion insulin (Actrapid) at 0.1 units/kg/hour. Wait until initial fluid is running and K+ known.
    - **Potassium:** Add to fluids immediately if K+ <5.5. If K+ <2.5, give 0.5 mmol/kg over 1 hour before starting insulin.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
    - **Monitoring:**
        - Hourly: BG, K+, pH/HCO3 (VBG), GCS, pupil size, HR, BP, RR, SpO₂
        - Every 2 hours: urine output, weight (if possible)
    - **Fluid adjustment:** Switch to 0.9% NaCl + 5% dextrose + KCl when BG ≤14 mmol/L. Continue insulin at same rate until acidosis resolved (pH >7.3, HCO3 >15). Do not stop insulin if BG normal – add dextrose.
    - **Two-bag system:** One bag with 0.9% saline + KCl, another with 10% dextrose + KCl – adjust rate to keep glucose 7–14 mmol/L.
    - **Cerebral oedema monitoring:** Any neuro deterioration – call senior/ICU. Give IV mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg or hypertonic saline 2.7–3% (3 mL/kg) and raise head of bed.
    - **No bicarbonate** unless pH <6.9 (rare, controversial – discuss with PICU).
    - **Transition to subcutaneous insulin:** When acidosis resolved and child eating/drinking. Give first SC dose before stopping IV insulin (overlap 30–60 min).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - New-onset T1DM: referral to paediatric diabetes team for education (carb counting, injection technique, sick day rules).
    - Existing T1DM: review precipitant (intercurrent illness, missed insulin, pump failure) and reinforce sick day management.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - **IV fluids:** 0.9% NaCl + KCl [rate per local fluid protocol – total 48h deficit + maintenance]
    - **VTE prophylaxis:** Not routine in paediatric DKA unless adolescent with additional risk factors (obesity, central line) – discuss with senior
    - **Diet/NBM:** NBM until acidosis resolves and child alert. Then clear fluids → normal diet when stable
    - **Observations:** Hourly for first 6–12 hours, then every 2 hours if stable
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line insulin:** Soluble insulin (Actrapid) IV infusion. Dose: 0.1 units/kg/hour (do not give IV bolus). Continue until acidosis resolved.
- **Potassium replacement:** Add KCl to IV fluids. [Check local policy – typical: 40 mmol/L if K+ 2.5–5.5; 20 mmol/L if K+ 5.5–6.0; do not add if K+ >6.0]. If K+ <2.5: give 0.5 mmol/kg KCl over 1 hour before insulin.
- **Fluids:** 0.9% NaCl (first hour ± 10 mL/kg bolus). Then 0.9% NaCl + 5–10% dextrose + KCl.
- **Cerebral oedema:** Mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV over 10–15 min OR hypertonic saline (2.7% NaCl) 3 mL/kg IV over 15 min. Both are emergency treatments – give before CT.
- **Contraindications / interactions:**
    - Insulin: do not give with low K+ (<3.0) after cardiac monitor
    - Potassium: monitor ECG; avoid rapid IV push (fatal arrhythmia)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None specific; but in renal impairment, potassium must be even more cautious.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** *Giving insulin before checking baseline potassium* – fatal hypokalaemia. Also: *over-rapid fluid boluses (>20 mL/kg initial)* predisposing to cerebral oedema.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
    - Cerebral oedema (peak at 6–12 hours after treatment starts)
    - Hypokalaemia, hyperkalaemia (from rapid correction)
    - Hypoglycaemia (if dextrose not added or insulin continued without glucose)
    - Acute kidney injury (from severe dehydration)
    - Thrombosis (central lines, hyperosmolality)
- **Chronic:**
    - Long-term microvascular complications (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy) if poor glycaemic control after episode
sections.Prognosis: - Mortality <0.5% in developed countries with prompt care; cerebral oedema mortality ~25% and survivors often have neurological deficits. Most children with new-onset T1DM recover fully with good education and insulin therapy.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Do** hourly neurological observations (GCS, pupils, vitals) – the only way to catch cerebral oedema early. Document each hour.
- **Do** use a two-bag system (saline + dextrose) once glucose <14 mmol/L – prevents hypoglycaemia while continuing insulin to clear ketones.
- **Do** suspect new-onset T1DM if a child with no known diabetes presents with DKA – check HbA1c, but more importantly start insulin and refer to diabetes team.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do NOT** give an IV bolus of insulin – it increases risk of hypokalaemia and cerebral oedema without benefit.
- **Do NOT** aim for rapid correction of acidosis – correcting too quickly worsens cerebral oedema. Slow and steady wins.
- **Do NOT** stop insulin when glucose normalises – instead, add dextrose to fluids. Ketone clearance depends on insulin.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** New headache, vomiting, confusion, drowsiness, or seizure (cerebral oedema can occur up to 24h post-treatment). Also if unable to keep down fluids/insulin.
- **Return if:** Blood ketones >1.5 mmol/L on home meter, or blood glucose persistently >15 despite correction.
- **Follow-up:** With paediatric diabetes team within 48–72 hours. Teach sick day rules (never stop insulin, increase ketone monitoring, contact team if unwell).
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior / ICU immediately if:**
    - Any GCS drop (even 1 point)
    - New focal neurology or seizure
    - Cushing’s triad (bradycardia + hypertension + irregular breathing)
    - pH <6.9 (discuss PICU/admission)
    - Persistent hypoglycaemia <3 mmol/L despite stopping insulin
    - Hypernatraemia >155 mmol/L (risk of cerebral oedema)
- **Use paediatric early warning score (PEWS) – escalate per local protocol if PEWS triggers.** General thresholds: HR >170, RR >60, SpO₂ <92%, GCS <15.

rawMarkdown:
## Paediatric DKA
**One-liner:** Acute metabolic emergency in children with type 1 diabetes (T1DM) – hyperglycaemia, ketonaemia, acidosis – risk of cerebral oedema within first hours.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Cerebral oedema** – the top killer in paediatric DKA. Single finding: *headache + GCS drop* (even 1–2 points) or *bradycardia + hypertension* (Cushing’s reflex). Must be anticipated, not responded to after the fact.
- **Hypokalaemia** – fatal arrhythmia if insulin is given without adequate potassium replacement. Single finding: *U waves on cardiac monitor* or *low K+ on blood gas* (<3.0 mmol/L before starting insulin). Rule out by checking baseline K+ before any insulin.
- **Hypoglycaemia** – rapid drop from aggressive insulin without dextrose backup. Single finding: *glucose <4 mmol/L on hourly BM* while insulin is still running. Prevent by switching to 10% dextrose when glucose reaches 14 mmol/L.

### Presentation
- Polydipsia, polyuria, nocturia (or enuresis in toilet-trained children) – often weeks of symptoms
- Weight loss (can be dramatic)
- Vomiting, abdominal pain (may mimic surgical abdomen)
- Dehydration signs: dry mucous membranes, reduced skin turgor, sunken eyes, prolonged capillary refill
- Kussmaul breathing – deep, sighing respirations (compensatory respiratory alkalosis)
- Fruity-smelling breath (acetone)
- Altered consciousness (ranging from drowsy to coma – indicate cerebral oedema or severe acidosis)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Hyperosmolar hyperglycaemic state (HHS)** – rare in children; differentiate: pH >7.3, HCO3 >15, low or absent ketones, very high glucose (>30–40 mmol/L) and osmolality
- **Sepsis (e.g., pneumonia, gastroenteritis)** – can cause acidosis and vomiting; differentiate: no marked polyuria/weight loss, ketones may be present but blood glucose normal
- **Surgical abdomen (appendicitis, intussusception)** – abdominal pain with vomiting; differentiate: check glucose and ketones – if elevated, DKA is likely the cause
- **Salicylate poisoning** – can cause metabolic acidosis with respiratory alkalosis and vomiting; differentiate: no hyperglycaemia, no ketones, history of ingestion
- **New-onset T1DM without DKA** – mild polyuria/polydipsia, normal pH/HCO3; just need insulin start as outpatient

**Splitter:** *Blood pH <7.3 + serum ketones >3 mmol/L = DKA, not HHS or sepsis.*

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour):**
    - Capillary blood glucose (BG) – always from fingerstick (if inaccurate, use venous)
    - Urine/serum ketones (bedside meter – β-hydroxybutyrate preferred; >1.5 mmol/L confirms ketosis)
    - ECG (look for U waves, peaked T waves – hyperkalaemia; flat/broad T waves – hypokalaemia)
    - Hourly neurological observations (GCS, pupil size/reaction, vital signs) – start immediately
- **Bloods:**
    - Venous blood gas (VBG) for pH, HCO3, base deficit – arteriorial not needed
    - Serum electrolytes: Na, K, Cl, Ca, Mg (corrected Na: Na + 2×(glucose−5.6)/5.6)
    - Urea, creatinine, osmolality (calculated or measured)
    - Full blood count (WBC may be raised – stress/demargination, not infection)
    - Blood cultures if febrile or shock
    - HbA1c (confirms T1DM diagnosis, but not needed acutely)
- **Imaging:** Not routine. Only if suspected cerebral oedema: CT head (after clinical stabilisation) to rule out other causes of neuro deterioration – but treatment for cerebral oedema should not wait for CT.
- **Diagnostic criteria (ISPAD/BSPED):**
    - Hyperglycaemia: plasma glucose >11 mmol/L
    - Acidosis: venous pH <7.3 OR HCO3 <15 mmol/L
    - Ketonaemia: serum β-hydroxybutyrate ≥3 mmol/L or urine ketones ≥3+
- **Never-miss test:** *Hourly GCS + pupillary exam* – the earliest sign of cerebral oedema. Also: *baseline potassium before starting insulin* – fatal hypokalaemia if ignored.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
    - **ABC:** Secure airway if GCS <8 or progressive deterioration. Give 100% oxygen if shocked/seizure.
    - **IV access:** Two wide-bore cannulae. Take blood before any fluid.
    - **Fluid resuscitation:**
        - If shocked (cap refill >2 s, weak pulses, tachycardia, hypotension): give 10 mL/kg 0.9% NaCl over 15–30 min. Reassess. Maximum 20 mL/kg unless severe shock.
        - Dehydration calculation: use clinical estimate (mild 5%, moderate 7.5%, severe 10%).
        - Deficit replaced over 48 hours – do NOT aim for rapid correction (risk of cerebral oedema). Use 0.9% NaCl ± 5% dextrose (once glucose <14 mmol/L).
        - Add potassium chloride [check local policy – typically 40 mmol/L unless K+ <2.5, then higher]
    - **Insulin:** Do NOT give IV bolus. Start IV infusion insulin (Actrapid) at 0.1 units/kg/hour. Wait until initial fluid is running and K+ known.
    - **Potassium:** Add to fluids immediately if K+ <5.5. If K+ <2.5, give 0.5 mmol/kg over 1 hour before starting insulin.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
    - **Monitoring:**
        - Hourly: BG, K+, pH/HCO3 (VBG), GCS, pupil size, HR, BP, RR, SpO₂
        - Every 2 hours: urine output, weight (if possible)
    - **Fluid adjustment:** Switch to 0.9% NaCl + 5% dextrose + KCl when BG ≤14 mmol/L. Continue insulin at same rate until acidosis resolved (pH >7.3, HCO3 >15). Do not stop insulin if BG normal – add dextrose.
    - **Two-bag system:** One bag with 0.9% saline + KCl, another with 10% dextrose + KCl – adjust rate to keep glucose 7–14 mmol/L.
    - **Cerebral oedema monitoring:** Any neuro deterioration – call senior/ICU. Give IV mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg or hypertonic saline 2.7–3% (3 mL/kg) and raise head of bed.
    - **No bicarbonate** unless pH <6.9 (rare, controversial – discuss with PICU).
    - **Transition to subcutaneous insulin:** When acidosis resolved and child eating/drinking. Give first SC dose before stopping IV insulin (overlap 30–60 min).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
    - New-onset T1DM: referral to paediatric diabetes team for education (carb counting, injection technique, sick day rules).
    - Existing T1DM: review precipitant (intercurrent illness, missed insulin, pump failure) and reinforce sick day management.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**
    - **IV fluids:** 0.9% NaCl + KCl [rate per local fluid protocol – total 48h deficit + maintenance]
    - **VTE prophylaxis:** Not routine in paediatric DKA unless adolescent with additional risk factors (obesity, central line) – discuss with senior
    - **Diet/NBM:** NBM until acidosis resolves and child alert. Then clear fluids → normal diet when stable
    - **Observations:** Hourly for first 6–12 hours, then every 2 hours if stable

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line insulin:** Soluble insulin (Actrapid) IV infusion. Dose: 0.1 units/kg/hour (do not give IV bolus). Continue until acidosis resolved.
- **Potassium replacement:** Add KCl to IV fluids. [Check local policy – typical: 40 mmol/L if K+ 2.5–5.5; 20 mmol/L if K+ 5.5–6.0; do not add if K+ >6.0]. If K+ <2.5: give 0.5 mmol/kg KCl over 1 hour before insulin.
- **Fluids:** 0.9% NaCl (first hour ± 10 mL/kg bolus). Then 0.9% NaCl + 5–10% dextrose + KCl.
- **Cerebral oedema:** Mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV over 10–15 min OR hypertonic saline (2.7% NaCl) 3 mL/kg IV over 15 min. Both are emergency treatments – give before CT.
- **Contraindications / interactions:**
    - Insulin: do not give with low K+ (<3.0) after cardiac monitor
    - Potassium: monitor ECG; avoid rapid IV push (fatal arrhythmia)
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** None specific; but in renal impairment, potassium must be even more cautious.
- **Most dangerous dose error:** *Giving insulin before checking baseline potassium* – fatal hypokalaemia. Also: *over-rapid fluid boluses (>20 mL/kg initial)* predisposing to cerebral oedema.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
    - Cerebral oedema (peak at 6–12 hours after treatment starts)
    - Hypokalaemia, hyperkalaemia (from rapid correction)
    - Hypoglycaemia (if dextrose not added or insulin continued without glucose)
    - Acute kidney injury (from severe dehydration)
    - Thrombosis (central lines, hyperosmolality)
- **Chronic:**
    - Long-term microvascular complications (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy) if poor glycaemic control after episode

### Prognosis
- Mortality <0.5% in developed countries with prompt care; cerebral oedema mortality ~25% and survivors often have neurological deficits. Most children with new-onset T1DM recover fully with good education and insulin therapy.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Do** hourly neurological observations (GCS, pupils, vitals) – the only way to catch cerebral oedema early. Document each hour.
- **Do** use a two-bag system (saline + dextrose) once glucose <14 mmol/L – prevents hypoglycaemia while continuing insulin to clear ketones.
- **Do** suspect new-onset T1DM if a child with no known diabetes presents with DKA – check HbA1c, but more importantly start insulin and refer to diabetes team.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do NOT** give an IV bolus of insulin – it increases risk of hypokalaemia and cerebral oedema without benefit.
- **Do NOT** aim for rapid correction of acidosis – correcting too quickly worsens cerebral oedema. Slow and steady wins.
- **Do NOT** stop insulin when glucose normalises – instead, add dextrose to fluids. Ketone clearance depends on insulin.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** New headache, vomiting, confusion, drowsiness, or seizure (cerebral oedema can occur up to 24h post-treatment). Also if unable to keep down fluids/insulin.
- **Return if:** Blood ketones >1.5 mmol/L on home meter, or blood glucose persistently >15 despite correction.
- **Follow-up:** With paediatric diabetes team within 48–72 hours. Teach sick day rules (never stop insulin, increase ketone monitoring, contact team if unwell).

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior / ICU immediately if:**
    - Any GCS drop (even 1 point)
    - New focal neurology or seizure
    - Cushing’s triad (bradycardia + hypertension + irregular breathing)
    - pH <6.9 (discuss PICU/admission)
    - Persistent hypoglycaemia <3 mmol/L despite stopping insulin
    - Hypernatraemia >155 mmol/L (risk of cerebral oedema)
- **Use paediatric early warning score (PEWS) – escalate per local protocol if PEWS triggers.** General thresholds: HR >170, RR >60, SpO₂ <92%, GCS <15.
